rationale: The page contains detailed educational content on three distinct topics: Reverse Faulting, Pacific Ring of Fire, and Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC). It includes definitions, causes, effects, and geographical context, making it highly suitable for generating multiple questions.
cleanedText:
About Reverse Faulting
• Reverse faulting (or thrust faulting) occurs when a block of Earth's crust is pushed up over another due to compressional forces.
• “Shallow” Reverse faulting means rupture occurred near the Earth's surface. It can cause strong ground shaking, powerful aftershocks, tsunamis, and damage to infrastructure.
Diagram: Reverse Fault. Normal fault, Extension, Reverse fault, Compression, Fault scarp.
Pacific Ring of Fire
• Also called Circum-Pacific Belt, it is a horseshoe-shaped belt around the rim of the Pacific Ocean characterized by a high number of active and dormant volcanoes and frequent earthquakes.
o Accounts for ~75% of the world’s active volcanoes and ~90% of the world’s earthquakes.
• Reasons for formation of Pacific Ring of Fire
o Plate Tectonics:
• Convergent plate boundaries between the Pacific Plate and other plates like Indo-Australian Plates, Juan de Fuca etc. create subduction zones, leading to volcanic activity.
• Divergent Boundaries create seafloor spreading and rift valleys. E.g. East Pacific Rise, where Pacific Plate diverges from Cocos, Nazca, and Antarctic Plates, featuring volcanism and hydrothermal vents.
o Earth’s deepest ocean trenches (e.g. Mariana Trench) are located along Subduction zones, causing deep earthquakes.
• Earthquakes occur along the trenches and faults as the two plates scrape against each other. E.g. transform boundary at San Andreas Fault (California).
Diagram: Ring of Fire. Trenches shown: Kuril Trench, Japan Trench, Ryukyu Trench, Challenger Deep, Java (Sunda) Trench, Bougainville Trench, Tonga Trench, Kermadec Trench, Puerto Rico Trench, Middle America Trench, Peru-Chile Trench, South Sandwich Trench.
6.7. ATLANTIC MERIDIONAL OVERTURNING CIRCULATION (AMOC)
Why in the news?
A scientific reconstruction of 12,000 Years (Holocene Epoch) of Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) showed that after remaining largely stable (natural fluctuation) for most of the period, modern AMOC is showing unprecedented weakening.
AMOC
• A system of ocean currents that circulates water within the Atlantic Ocean, bringing warm water north and cold water south.
• Driving Force: Differences in water temperature and salinity.
o Warm tropical water moves towards the poles, becomes cool & dense, sinks in the North Atlantic and flows south before warming and resurfacing thus restarting the cycle.
Diagram: Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC). High salinity water cools and sinks in the North Atlantic. Warm surface flow. Cool subsurface flow. Continents: North America, South America, Europe, Africa, Asia, Australia. Oceans: Atlantic Ocean, Indian Ocean, Pacific Ocean.
